title: Closing the gap between applied AI engineering and ML fundamentals
excerpt: Six years of shipping production agentic systems taught me how to build with LLMs. It didn't teach me why the maths underneath them works — so I'm fixing that, in the open.
body:
## Why write in the open

Most of my technical growth over the last few years has come from shipping — multi-agent platforms, retrieval pipelines, fine-tuned classifiers running in production. That's real experience, but it's also narrow in a specific way: applied engineering optimises for *what works*, not always *why*.

This is the first post in a series where I close that gap deliberately — working through fundamentals (attention from scratch, policy gradient methods, reward modelling) and writing up what I learn, including the parts I get wrong the first time.

### What's next

- A transformer implemented from scratch, no framework shortcuts
- Notes on PPO vs DPO for preference optimisation
- Reward modelling experiments on small open datasets

If you're on a similar path — applied engineer moving toward research-level fundamentals — I'd like to hear what worked for you.

title: [REDACTED]
excerpt: Notes from building a system that tunes its own retrieval and generation parameters using time-decayed reward signals — and the failure modes nobody warns you about.
body:
## The problem

Static RAG configurations degrade as usage patterns shift. Retrieval strategies tuned for one cohort of queries quietly stop working for another, and nobody notices until [REDACTED].

[REDACTED], learning from real user feedback with [REDACTED] — recent signal matters more than signal from three months ago.

### [REDACTED]

- **Reward attribution lag.** Feedback doesn't arrive instantly, and naive attribution assigns credit to the wrong action if you're not careful with windowing.
- **Cold-start variance.** New configurations get explored aggressively before there's enough signal, which briefly makes the system look worse before it looks better.
- **Silent drift in the reward model itself.** If the thing scoring quality is an LLM, it needs its own monitoring — otherwise you're tuning against a moving target.

More on the fixes for each of these in the next post.
